patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "hey gabriel i'm doctor scott good to see you today i know you've heard about dax is it okay if i tell dax a little bit about you"] sure

[Clinician asked: "okay so gabriel is a 43 -year-old male today here for back pain evaluation and also has a past medical history of diabetes high blood pressure and high cholesterol so gabriel tell me what's going on with your back"] well i was working in the yard and you know bent over to pick something up and i got this pain and you know across the lower part of my back and then it went down my left leg and you know it's been going on for about four days and just does n't seem to be getting any better

[Clinician asked: "okay are you a big gardener or this is something that you just started working in the yard"] yeah i know my wife held a gun to my head make me go out there work in the yard and carry some stuff around it's not my not my first choice but
[Clinician asked: "sure sure"] but that day i i lost the i lost the argument

[Clinician asked: "yeah yeah that happens to all of this so when this back pain happened so it was basically you were lifting you were bending down to lift something up and you had the sharp pain going down your right leg you said"] left leg
[Clinician asked: "left leg okay got it sorry and any weakness or numbness in your legs or just the pain mostly"] in in certain positions i get some tingling but no mostly just pain
[Clinician asked: "okay and any loss of bowel or bladder function at all or anything like that"] no
[Clinician asked: "okay and have you had any back surgeries or back problems in the past or this is kind of the first time"] no surgeries you know i've i've had back pain occasionally over the years
[Clinician asked: "okay have you had any any have you tried anything for pain for this have you tried any any medications at all"] i've had ibuprofen it it helped some

[Clinician asked: "okay got it alright well i'll i'll examine you in a second but before we do that let's talk about some of the other conditions that we're kinda following you for i'm looking at your problem list now and you've got a history of diabetes and you're on metformin five hundred milligram twice a day and y"] yeah i i check my sugar two or three times a week most of the time it's in that one twenty to one forty range
[Clinician asked: "okay"] yeah i take my medicine okay my diet is alright you know i could be fifteen pounds lighter that would be alright but
[Clinician asked: "sure"] i i i think the sugar has been okay

[Clinician asked: "okay we checked your hemoglobin a1c last time i'm looking at your records in epic and it showed that it was you know seven . one so it's it's it's good but it could be better any you know we talked about it controlling your diet or improving your diet and trying to have a balanced meal and not eatin"] yeah she still makes cookies and i still eat them but you know we are trying to trying to do better trying to stay away from more of those carbs and focus on you know less carby less sweet stuff

[Clinician asked: "okay alright yeah that's always a struggle i certainly understand but you know really important with your diabetes just to prevent some of the complications like kidney failure and eye problems and just keep your sugar under balance so i'll order another hemoglobin a1c today we'll check that again t"] okay

[Clinician asked: "for your high blood pressure your blood pressure in the clinic looks pretty good it's about one twenty over seventy right now we have you on norvasc five milligrams once a day how are things going with that are you are you checking that periodically or any issues with that at all"] yeah i guess i check it maybe once a week or two or three times a month and it it the vast majority of the time when i check it it's good usually either that one twenty to one thirty over seventy to eighty range i i think the blood pressure's okay
[Clinician asked: "okay"] i have n't had any real problems there i i have had some some swelling in my ankles though
[Clinician asked: "okay is that new or is that been going on for a while"] well it it started maybe i do n't know a month or two after i started the norvasc
[Clinician asked: "okay"] and i was just wondering if the two might be related

[Clinician asked: "yeah i mean certainly it could be it is you know sometimes that medication can cause that so i'll i'll examine you in a second and see if we need to make any modifications okay"] okay
[Clinician asked: "alright so and your anything else bothering you today"] no i'm we're doing okay i think

[Clinician asked: "so let me examine you for a second i'm gon na go ahead and gabriel i'm gon na do my magic exam now let's pretend i i'm just gon na verbalize some of my findings as i do my exam and so"] these are like my video visit exams

[Clinician asked: "exactly so your neck exam has no jvd there is no bruits that i can hear your lung exam no rales no wheezing on your heart exam you do have a two over six systolic ejection murmur you had that in the past so i'm not too worried about that otherwise regular rate and rhythm on your heart exam on your o"] no

[Clinician asked: "okay for the diabetes the the second problem that we talked about today i'm gon na order another hemoglobin a1c continue the metformin five hundred milligrams twice a day why do n't we have you come back in about two weeks and we should have some of the results back and we can discuss if we need to "] no i i think i'm okay you gave me a year's worth of refills last time we were together
[Clinician asked: "okay sounds great alright thanks gabriel good seeing you again"] good to see you thanks
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Back pain evaluation.

MEDICAL HISTORY

The patient has a history of diabetes, hypertension, and high cholesterol.

SURGICAL HISTORY

He denies previous back surgeries.

MEDICATIONS

The patient currently utilizes metformin 500 mg twice daily and Norvasc.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Cardiovascular: Reports ankle swelling.
Musculoskeletal: Reports lower back pain.
Neurological: Reports tingling in left leg. Denies weakness or numbness in legs, or loss of bowl or bladder function.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Neurological
- Orientation: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Normal gait.

Neck
- General Examination: No JVD. No bruits.

Respiratory
- Auscultation of Lungs: No wheezes, rales.

Cardiovascular
- Auscultation of Heart: Regular rate and rhythm. 2/6 systolic ejection murmur. Otherwise, regular rate and rhythm.

Musculoskeletal
- Examination: Tenderness on the left paraspinal area. Straight leg raise test is negative. Reflexes are normal. 1+ nonpitting edema of lower extremities. No calf tenderness. Negative Homan's sign.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Lower back pain.
- Medical Reasoning: I believe this is more of a muscular sprain.
- Patient Education and Counseling: We discussed treatment options today.
- Medical Treatment: I am going to start him on Naprosyn 500 mg twice a day and Flexeril 10 mg twice a day. I will refer him for physical therapy to help strengthen some of the muscles in his lower back.
- Additional Testing: I do not think he needs an x-ray at this stage, however if he does not improve, we will order one for further evaluation.

2. Diabetes.
- Medical Reasoning: His most recent A1c was 7.1 and his blood sugar levels are typically between 120-140.
- Patient Education and Counseling: We discussed treatment options today.
- Medical Treatment: He will continue the metformin 500 mg twice a day.
- Additional Testing: I am going to order another hemoglobin A1c.

3. Hypertension.
- Medical Reasoning: He does have 1+ edema in his legs.
- Patient Education and Counseling: We discussed treatment options today. I explained that his edema is likely caused by the Norvasc.
- Medical Treatment: The patient will discontinue the use of Norvasc and we will start him on hydrochlorothiazide 10 mg once a day.
- Additional Testing: I will order a CBC and BMP. Additionally, I will order a repeat EKG, as well as a chest x-ray.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up in 2 weeks.

naive.response_text:
Gabriel, I understand how frustrating and uncomfortable this back pain must be. It’s important to take it seriously, especially since it’s impacting your daily activities. Based on your description and the exam, it sounds like we’re considering possibilities like a muscle strain or possibly something more specific, such as a herniated disc, given the leg pain. The good news is that the exam didn’t show any immediate red flags, but if the pain persists or worsens, we may need to explore further options, like imaging or other treatments.

Regarding the swelling in your ankles, we’ll keep an eye on that, especially since it started after starting norvasc. If it continues or gets worse, we can explore other options or adjustments. In the meantime, if the swelling bothers you, consider elevating your legs or using compression stockings. 

You’re doing a great job managing your diabetes and blood pressure. Keep up the good work with your diet and medications. If you have any questions or concerns in the meantime, don’t hesitate to reach out. We’re here to support you and help manage any side effects or concerns you have. Take care, and I hope you feel better soon.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Gabriel, thank you for sharing all this with me. It sounds like the back pain you're experiencing has been quite uncomfortable, especially with how it's been radiating down your leg. I can understand why you're concerned—it's not fun dealing with pain that doesn't let up, and it can really interfere with your daily life. I want to make sure we get to the bottom of this and help you feel better.

We've talked about your back pain and the possible causes, like muscle strain or something that might need a bit more attention. Since the pain has been going on for a few days and isn't improving, I think it would be a good idea to consider imaging, like an MRI, to get a clearer picture of what's going on. For now, we'll continue with the ibuprofen, but if it doesn't help or if your symptoms get worse, we can explore other options together.

Regarding the swelling in your ankles, it's possible it could be related to your blood pressure medication. We'll keep an eye on it and adjust your treatment if needed to make sure you're comfortable. For your diabetes, we'll check your A1C in a couple of weeks to see how things are going and adjust your management plan if necessary.

Let's plan to follow up in two weeks to review the results of the imaging and the A1C. Before you go, could you summarize what we've discussed so far? That way, I can make sure everything is clear and you feel confident about the next steps.

full.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns about your back pain and swelling in your ankles. I understand how these symptoms might be affecting your daily life.

The back pain you're experiencing, along with the pain radiating down your left leg and tingling in certain positions, suggests we should consider possible causes like nerve compression or muscle strain. We'll need to monitor your condition closely over the next few weeks to see if there are any changes.

Swelling in your ankles could be related to your medication, norvasc. It's important to monitor this as it might indicate fluid retention or other underlying issues. We'll keep a close eye on this to ensure it doesn't develop into something more serious.

Losing fifteen pounds is a great goal. Group exercise programs can be really helpful for chronic conditions and can improve overall health outcomes. We'll support you in finding a program that fits your needs and lifestyle.

We'll monitor your back pain and leg symptoms closely over the next four weeks.

We'll keep an eye on the swelling in your ankles and adjust your medication if necessary.
